_meta.notes: Add more questions to each array. MCQ target: 800+. Session draws 40 MCQ + 12 DD + 8 CS sub-questions per run.

mcq.q: Which function is generally delegable across most major data sources in Power Apps?
options: Search
Filter
CountIf
GroupBy
mcq.explanation: Filter pushes its predicate to the server on Dataverse, SharePoint, and SQL Server. Only matching records are returned to the client.
wrongExplanations: Search() is non-delegable on most sources including SharePoint — it retrieves a limited record set locally before searching.
CountIf is non-delegable — it counts only within the locally-fetched record set (max 500–2000).
GroupBy is a non-delegable data-shaping function that operates entirely client-side.

mcq.q: You need to retrieve a single matching record from a large Dataverse table efficiently. Which function should you use?
options: First
Search
LookUp
Last
mcq.explanation: LookUp is delegable for single-record retrieval — Dataverse processes the condition server-side and returns just the one matching record.
wrongExplanations: First() is non-delegable. It fetches the first page of records locally and picks from those — may not return the true first record in a large dataset.
Search() is non-delegable on Dataverse and SharePoint — it operates on locally-fetched records only.
Last() is non-delegable and has the same limitation as First() — it operates on the local record page, not the full dataset.

mcq.q: Which text function is the recommended delegable replacement for Search()?
options: EndsWith inside Filter
StartsWith inside Filter
Len inside Filter
Mid inside Filter
mcq.explanation: StartsWith inside Filter delegates the text-prefix check to the server and is the standard delegable pattern for search bars.
wrongExplanations: EndsWith is non-delegable — unlike StartsWith, suffix matching cannot be efficiently pushed to most data sources.
Len() is non-delegable and does not perform text searching — it returns string length and runs client-side.
Mid() is non-delegable. Substring extraction from an arbitrary position cannot be delegated.

mcq.q: Which logical operators are delegable in Power Apps?
options: Not (!)
And (&&) and Or (||)
All logical operators are delegable
No logical operators are delegable
mcq.explanation: And (&&) and Or (||) are delegable across most major data sources, allowing compound filter conditions to be pushed to the server.
wrongExplanations: Not (!) has delegation limitations, particularly on SharePoint — it cannot be pushed to the SharePoint server.
Not (!) is a notable exception — it is not fully delegable on all sources.
And and Or are fully delegable. Only Not has limitations.

msRef.label: Operators and Identifiers in Power Apps

mcq.q: Sum, Avg, Min, and Max are only delegable when using which data source?
options: SharePoint
Dataverse
SQL Server
Salesforce
mcq.explanation: SQL Server is the only connector that supports server-side delegation of Sum, Avg, Min, and Max in Power Apps canvas apps.
wrongExplanations: SharePoint does not delegate aggregation functions — Sum, Avg, Min, Max all run client-side against the fetched record page.
Dataverse does not delegate these aggregation functions in canvas apps. Use Power Automate or dataflows for Dataverse aggregations.
Salesforce does not delegate Sum, Avg, Min, or Max in Power Apps canvas apps.

msRef.label: Delegation for SQL Server in Power Apps

mcq.q: What is the default data row limit for non-delegable queries in Power Apps?
mcq.explanation: The default data row limit is 500 records. Non-delegable operations only process this fetched set, potentially missing thousands of records.
wrongExplanations: 100 is not a valid limit. The default is 500.
1000 is a configurable value between 500 and 2000, but not the default.
2000 is the maximum configurable limit, not the default.

msRef.label: Change the limit for non-delegable queries

mcq.q: What is the MAXIMUM data row limit you can configure for non-delegable queries?
mcq.explanation: The maximum configurable data row limit is 2000 records. Beyond this, proper delegation is required.
wrongExplanations: 500 is only the default — it can be raised. Not the maximum.
1000 is a valid setting but not the maximum. You can go up to 2000.
10000 is not a supported limit. The platform caps the configurable limit at 2000.

msRef.label: Change the limit for non-delegable queries

mcq.q: Collect() and ClearCollect() are non-delegable. What is unusual about their behaviour in Power Apps Studio?
options: They auto-paginate to get all records
They do not show a delegation warning despite being non-delegable
They only work with Dataverse
They raise two delegation warnings
mcq.explanation: Collect and ClearCollect are non-delegable but silently so — Power Apps Studio does not underline them with a delegation warning, making the data limit easy to miss.
wrongExplanations: Collect and ClearCollect do not auto-paginate. They load up to the row limit and stop — records beyond are silently excluded.
They work with any tabular data source, not just Dataverse.
They show no delegation warning at all — the absence of a warning is exactly the gotcha.

mcq.q: Which of the following is classified as NON-delegable?
options: Filter
LookUp
SortByColumns
CountIf
mcq.explanation: CountIf is non-delegable — it only counts records within the locally-fetched page (default 500, max 2000).
wrongExplanations: Filter is one of the most reliably delegable functions in Power Apps.
LookUp is delegable for single-record retrieval on most major data sources.
SortByColumns is a delegable sort function that pushes ordering to the data source server.

mcq.q: First, FirstN, Last, LastN are non-delegable because:
options: They are not supported on SharePoint
They operate on the locally-fetched record page, not the full dataset
They conflict with SortByColumns
They require a primary key column
mcq.explanation: First, FirstN, Last, LastN operate on the client-side fetched dataset. There is no way to instruct the data source to return just one positional record, so delegation is impossible.
wrongExplanations: Support on SharePoint is not the reason — these functions are non-delegable on all data sources.
There is no conflict with SortByColumns. The issue is purely that positional retrieval cannot be pushed to the server.
Primary key availability does not affect the delegation status of these functions.

mcq.q: Search() on SharePoint is non-delegable. What should you use instead?
options: Filter with EndsWith
Filter with StartsWith
LookUp with a text condition
SortByColumns then Filter
mcq.explanation: Filter with StartsWith is the delegation-safe replacement for Search() on SharePoint. It pushes the prefix filter to the server.
wrongExplanations: EndsWith is non-delegable — suffix matching cannot be delegated on SharePoint or most other sources.
LookUp is delegable for single-record retrieval but is not a replacement for search-bar text filtering.
Sorting before filtering does not fix the delegation issue — Search() remains non-delegable regardless.

mcq.q: AddColumns, DropColumns, ShowColumns, and RenameColumns are classified as:
options: Fully delegable
Fully non-delegable
Partially delegable
Only delegable in Dataverse
mcq.explanation: These are partially delegable. Inner formulas (like Filter or LookUp) can delegate, but the function's output row count is bounded by the data row limit.
wrongExplanations: These functions are not fully delegable — their output is capped at the row limit even if inner formulas delegate.
They are not fully non-delegable — the inner formula delegation is meaningful for performance.
The data source does not make these functions fully delegable. The output cap applies regardless.

mcq.q: You use AddColumns with LookUp to join two tables. What is the key limitation?
options: LookUp is non-delegable inside AddColumns
LookUp delegates but AddColumns caps the output at the data row limit
Both fully delegate with no row limit
The join only works with fewer than 100 records
mcq.explanation: The LookUp inside AddColumns delegates, but AddColumns itself bounds the output rows to the configured data row limit.
wrongExplanations: LookUp is delegable and works inside AddColumns. The row cap is on AddColumns output, not on LookUp.
AddColumns does not delegate fully — its output is limited to the data row limit (500–2000).
There is no 100-record cap. The limit is the configured data row limit.

mcq.q: On SharePoint, which logical operator has known delegation limitations?
options: And (&&)
Not (!)
All operators work equally on SharePoint
mcq.explanation: Not (!) has delegation limitations on SharePoint. The SharePoint connector does not support server-side negation.
wrongExplanations: And (&&) is fully delegable on SharePoint — compound filter conditions work server-side.
Or (||) is delegable on SharePoint — multi-condition filters using Or push to the server.
SharePoint has a known limitation with Not (!). The operators are not all equally supported.

mcq.q: Which data source provides the most comprehensive delegation support in Power Apps?
options: SharePoint
Excel (OneDrive)
Dataverse
SQL Server
mcq.explanation: Dataverse offers the broadest delegation support — Filter, LookUp, Sort, comparisons, and logical operators all work reliably.
wrongExplanations: SharePoint has several delegation gaps — Not (!), the 'in' operator, and certain comparison operators.
Excel (OneDrive) has very limited delegation support and is not suitable for large datasets.
SQL Server has strong delegation including aggregations, but Dataverse's native Power Platform integration makes it the most comprehensive overall.

mcq.q: What does a blue wavy underline on a formula in Power Apps Studio indicate?
options: A syntax error
The formula may produce incomplete results with large datasets
A premium connector is required
The data source is offline
mcq.explanation: A blue delegation warning underline means Power Apps cannot push that formula part to the data source — with large datasets results will be based only on the fetched page.
wrongExplanations: Syntax errors produce red underlines, not blue. Blue specifically signals a delegation limitation.
Premium connector requirements appear separately in settings, not as blue formula underlines.
Offline issues produce connection errors, not delegation warnings.

mcq.q: Collections created with ClearCollect() — do delegation limits apply when filtering them afterwards?
options: Yes — collections have the same row limit as data sources
Yes — only 500 collection records can be filtered
No — collections are in-memory so all loaded records are available
No — but only if the collection has fewer than 2000 items
mcq.explanation: Collections are in-memory. Once loaded, all records exist locally, so non-delegable functions operate on the full collection without a row-limit constraint.
wrongExplanations: The row limit applies during the ClearCollect load from the source, not to subsequent operations on the collection.
There is no 500-record filter cap on collections once they are in memory.
There is no 2000-item threshold for collection operations. Any collection can be fully operated on once loaded.

mcq.q: The 'in' operator for delegation is supported on which data sources?
options: SharePoint and Dataverse
SQL Server and Salesforce
Only Dataverse
All major data sources
mcq.explanation: The in operator is delegable on SQL Server and Salesforce. SharePoint and Dataverse do not support it for delegation.
wrongExplanations: SharePoint does not support delegation of the 'in' operator. Dataverse also lacks this support.
Dataverse does not support delegation of the 'in' operator in canvas apps.
SharePoint and Dataverse do not delegate 'in' — it is not universally supported.

mcq.q: RemoveIf and UpdateIf are non-delegable. What is the risk with large datasets?
options: The app crashes when dataset exceeds row limit
Only records within the fetched row limit are removed or updated — others are silently missed
They throw a delegation error at runtime
They work correctly but run slowly
mcq.explanation: RemoveIf and UpdateIf only operate on the fetched record set (default 500, max 2000). Records beyond the limit are silently unaffected.
wrongExplanations: The app does not crash — it silently processes only the fetched page.
No runtime delegation error is thrown — the silent incompleteness is precisely the danger.
They do not run slowly on the full dataset — they simply never process records beyond the row limit.

mcq.q: Why is First(Sort(Table, Column, Descending)) considered problematic with large datasets?
options: Sort is non-delegable on all sources
Sort delegates but First does not — the result is picked from the fetched sorted page, not the true dataset
Both are non-delegable
It causes an infinite loop
mcq.explanation: Sort delegates but First is non-delegable — Power Apps fetches the first sorted page and picks from that local set, potentially missing the true top record.
wrongExplanations: Sort and SortByColumns are both delegable on major sources. Sort is not the problem.
Sort does delegate — the issue is specifically First() being non-delegable.
There is no infinite loop. The formula silently returns a potentially incorrect result.

mcq.q: EndsWith() — how does its delegation status compare to StartsWith()?
options: Both are delegable
EndsWith is delegable; StartsWith is not
EndsWith is non-delegable; StartsWith is delegable
Both are non-delegable
mcq.explanation: StartsWith is delegable (servers can use prefix indexes) while EndsWith is non-delegable — suffix matching cannot be efficiently pushed to most data source servers.
wrongExplanations: Only StartsWith is delegable. EndsWith cannot be pushed because suffix-based indexes are not standard.
This is reversed. StartsWith is delegable; EndsWith is the non-delegable one.
StartsWith is delegable — it is specifically recommended as a server-side text search pattern.

mcq.q: GroupBy and Ungroup are non-delegable data-shaping functions. Which statement best explains why?
options: SharePoint does not support grouping
They restructure data already held in local memory — no server-side grouping instruction can be issued
They require a premium connector
They are deprecated in Power Apps
mcq.explanation: GroupBy and Ungroup restructure data that is already in the app's local memory. There is no equivalent server-side grouping operation that can be delegated.
wrongExplanations: The data source is not the reason — these functions are non-delegable on all sources including SQL Server and Dataverse.
No premium connector is required. The limitation is architectural, not licensing.
GroupBy and Ungroup are not deprecated — they are current functions, just non-delegable.

mcq.q: Filter(Products, Price >= 100 && Price <= 500) on Dataverse — what is the delegation status?
options: Non-delegable — && is not supported on Dataverse
Fully delegable — range filters with && delegate on Dataverse
Partially delegable — only one condition delegates
Non-delegable — range comparisons are not supported
mcq.explanation: On Dataverse, both comparison operators and && are delegable. This compound range filter is fully pushed to the server.
wrongExplanations: && (And) is fully delegable on Dataverse. Compound conditions are well supported.
Both conditions delegate together as a single compound predicate on Dataverse.
Range comparisons using >=, <= are among the most reliably delegable operations on Dataverse.

mcq.q: Suppressing a delegation warning in Power Apps Studio means:
options: The delegation issue is resolved
The warning is hidden but the data incompleteness risk remains unchanged
The function now delegates
Power Apps will auto-paginate to get all records
mcq.explanation: Suppressing only hides the visual indicator. The formula still does not delegate — the risk of incomplete results with large datasets is completely unchanged.
wrongExplanations: Suppression is cosmetic only. The non-delegation behaviour is completely unchanged.
Hiding the warning has no effect on how the formula executes.
Power Apps does not auto-paginate when warnings are suppressed.

mcq.q: Using Power Automate for aggregations on large datasets is recommended because:
options: Power Automate is always faster than Power Apps formulas
Power Automate runs logic server-side, bypassing Power Apps delegation row limits entirely
Power Automate automatically caches query results
Power Automate supports all non-delegable functions natively
mcq.explanation: Power Automate executes on Microsoft's servers, not in the browser. It can access the full dataset directly with no row-limit constraint.
wrongExplanations: Speed depends on the operation and latency. The benefit is full-dataset access, not guaranteed speed.
Power Automate does not automatically cache. Caching must be explicitly designed.
Power Automate uses action-based logic, not Power Apps formula functions.

msRef.label: Using Power Automate with Power Apps

mcq.q: Filter(Orders, CustomerID = Gallery1.Selected.CustomerID) on Dataverse — does the control reference prevent delegation?
options: Yes — control references always prevent delegation
No — the control value is resolved locally and passed as a parameter in the delegated query
It depends on the column type
Partially — the filter delegates but CustomerID must be indexed
mcq.explanation: Control references do not prevent delegation. Power Apps resolves the control value locally, then passes it as a literal parameter in the delegated server query.
wrongExplanations: Control references do not prevent delegation. The resolved value is embedded in the server query.
Column type affects which operations can delegate, not whether a control reference can be used.
There is no partial delegation here — Filter with a resolved value either fully delegates or not.

mcq.q: An app uses Filter(Inventory, Quantity < 10 || IsDiscontinued = true) on Dataverse. This is:
options: Non-delegable — || is not supported on Dataverse
Fully delegable — Or (||) and comparison operators all delegate on Dataverse
Partially delegable — only one condition delegates
Non-delegable — mixing types in Or is unsupported
mcq.explanation: Or (||), < and = all delegate on Dataverse. This compound condition is fully pushed to the server.
wrongExplanations: Or (||) is delegable on Dataverse. Only Not (!) has limitations on some sources.
Both conditions delegate together as a single server-side predicate.
Mixing numeric and boolean conditions in Or is valid and does not affect delegation.

mcq.q: What is the primary risk when using non-delegable functions against a 50,000-row SharePoint list?
options: The app crashes immediately
Results are silently incomplete — only 500–2000 records are processed
SharePoint returns a 429 Too Many Requests error
Power Apps charges extra for the API calls
mcq.explanation: Non-delegable functions produce silently incomplete results — the app shows no error while potentially missing 48,000+ records.
wrongExplanations: The app does not crash. The silent incompleteness is arguably worse — incorrect results with no error.
SharePoint does not return a rate-limit error. It returns up to the row limit silently.
Power Apps does not charge extra for non-delegable operations.

mcq.q: ClearCollect() is used to load a 10,000-row Dataverse table. What actually happens?
options: All 10,000 records are loaded into the collection
Only the configured row limit (default 500, max 2000) is loaded — the rest are silently excluded
An error is shown because the table is too large
ClearCollect auto-paginates to retrieve all records
mcq.explanation: ClearCollect is non-delegable — it loads only up to the configured row limit (default 500, max 2000) and stops silently.
wrongExplanations: ClearCollect does not retrieve all records from a large table. It is bounded by the data row limit.
No error is shown — the silent incompleteness makes this dangerous.
ClearCollect does not auto-paginate. Use Power Automate to retrieve all records if needed.

mcq.q: On SQL Server, which of these formulas is delegable?
options: GroupBy(Orders, 'Region', 'Sales', Sales)
Sum(Filter(Orders, Year=2024), Amount)
Last(Sort(Orders, Date))
ClearCollect(col, Orders)
mcq.explanation: On SQL Server, Sum inside Filter is fully delegable — SQL Server supports both the Filter predicate and Sum aggregation server-side.
wrongExplanations: GroupBy is non-delegable on all data sources including SQL Server.
Last() is non-delegable — even though Sort delegates, Last picks from the local sorted page.
ClearCollect is non-delegable and loads only up to the row limit from SQL Server.

mcq.q: Which statement is TRUE about delegation in Power Apps?
options: All Power Apps functions delegate to all data sources
Delegation support varies by both function and data source — always check the delegation list
Delegation only matters for apps with more than 10,000 records
Once an app is published, delegation behaviour is locked
mcq.explanation: Delegation support is a matrix of function × data source. What delegates on SQL Server may not on SharePoint. Always consult the Microsoft delegation list.
wrongExplanations: Delegation is not universal. Each function-source combination has its own status.
Even a few thousand records can cause incorrect results with non-delegable functions if they exceed the row limit.
Delegation behaviour is determined at runtime by the formula and connector — not locked at publish.

mcq.q: LookUp(Customers, Email = User().Email) on Dataverse — will this delegate?
options: No — User() is not supported in delegation
Yes — LookUp and = delegate on Dataverse; User().Email is resolved locally and passed as a value
Only if the Email column is indexed
Partially — LookUp delegates but User() prevents full delegation
mcq.explanation: Yes, this delegates. User().Email is evaluated locally to a string value, which is then passed as a literal in the delegated server query.
wrongExplanations: User() is evaluated locally and its string result is passed to the server — it does not block delegation.
Column indexing affects performance but not delegation eligibility on Dataverse.
There is no split delegation — the whole LookUp with the resolved email is a single delegable operation.

mcq.q: Which architecture is most appropriate for a canvas app targeting 50,000+ records on SharePoint?
options: Raise the row limit to 2000 and use ClearCollect throughout
Use Filter+StartsWith for queries; use Power Automate for aggregations
Use Search() — it handles large sets better than Filter on SharePoint
Use CountIf for analytics — it is delegable on SharePoint
mcq.explanation: The correct pattern combines Filter+StartsWith for delegable queries and Power Automate for server-side aggregations.
wrongExplanations: Raising the row limit to 2000 still leaves 48,000+ records unprocessed — not a solution.
Search() is non-delegable on SharePoint — larger lists make it worse, not better.
CountIf is non-delegable on SharePoint — it only counts the fetched page.

dragdrop.instruction: Classify each Power Apps function by its delegation status. Drag each item into the correct zone.
dragdrop.explanation: Filter and LookUp are delegable. CountIf, Search, and GroupBy are non-delegable. AddColumns is partially delegable — inner formulas can delegate but output is row-limited.

dragdrop.instruction: Match each text function to whether it is delegable or non-delegable for text searching on SharePoint.
dragdrop.explanation: StartsWith and Filter+StartsWith are delegable on SharePoint. EndsWith and Search() are non-delegable.

dragdrop.instruction: Classify each data source by its aggregation delegation support (Sum, Avg, Min, Max).
dragdrop.explanation: Only SQL Server supports delegation of Sum, Avg, Min, and Max. All other sources require client-side aggregation or Power Automate.

dragdrop.instruction: Classify each logical operator by whether it is delegable or has limitations on SharePoint.
dragdrop.explanation: And (&&) and Or (||) are fully delegable on SharePoint. Not (!) has known limitations and falls back to client-side evaluation.

dragdrop.instruction: Sort the following scenarios by where the data processing happens — on the server or in the browser.
dragdrop.explanation: Filter and LookUp delegate to the server. CountIf and ClearCollect run client-side on the locally-fetched record set.

dragdrop.instruction: Classify each scenario as producing COMPLETE results or potentially INCOMPLETE results on a 100,000-row dataset.
dragdrop.explanation: Filter+StartsWith and SortByColumns are delegable and return complete results. Search() on SharePoint and CountIf on any source are non-delegable and return only the fetched page.

dragdrop.instruction: Match each function to its correct record limit behaviour.
zones: No row limit — server processes all records

correctMapping.Filter on Dataverse: No row limit — server processes all records
correctMapping.Sum on SQL Server: No row limit — server processes all records

dragdrop.explanation: Delegable operations (Filter on Dataverse, Sum on SQL Server) are not row-limited. ClearCollect loads only up to the row limit; GroupBy runs on whatever is in the collection.

dragdrop.instruction: Classify each approach as the CORRECT or INCORRECT workaround for counting active records in a 50,000-row SharePoint list.
items: Power Automate flow that maintains a count
dragdrop.explanation: Only Power Automate can reliably count all records in a large SharePoint list. All Power Apps client-side approaches are limited to the fetched row set.

msRef.label: Using Power Automate with Power Apps

dragdrop.instruction: Classify each function as delegable or non-delegable for removing/updating records in Power Apps.
dragdrop.explanation: Remove and Patch operating on a single known record are safe. RemoveIf and UpdateIf are non-delegable — they only affect records within the fetched page.

dragdrop.instruction: Match each table-shaping function to the correct partial delegation description.
zones: Partially Delegable — inner formulas delegate, output is row-limited

correctMapping.AddColumns: Partially Delegable — inner formulas delegate, output is row-limited
correctMapping.DropColumns: Partially Delegable — inner formulas delegate, output is row-limited
correctMapping.ShowColumns: Partially Delegable — inner formulas delegate, output is row-limited
correctMapping.RenameColumns: Partially Delegable — inner formulas delegate, output is row-limited

dragdrop.explanation: All four table-shaping functions share the same partial delegation behaviour — inner formulas can delegate but output rows are bounded by the data row limit.

casestudy.title: The SharePoint List App
casestudy.scenario: A developer at a logistics company is building a canvas app for warehouse managers. The data source is a SharePoint list with 75,000 inventory records. The app needs to: 1. Let users search items by name using a text input 2. Display a count of 'Out of Stock' items in a summary label 3. Show the 10 most recently updated items in a gallery 4. Allow managers to mark items as 'Discontinued' in bulk The developer has set the data row limit to 2000 in app settings. They have implemented the following: • Search(Inventory, SearchInput.Text, 'ItemName') for the search gallery • CountIf(Inventory, Status='Out of Stock') for the summary label • FirstN(Sort(Inventory, Modified, Descending), 10) for the recent items gallery • UpdateIf(Inventory, Selected=true, {Status: 'Discontinued'}) for bulk updates

questions.q: The search gallery using Search(Inventory, SearchInput.Text, 'ItemName') will produce which result for a user searching for 'Bolt'?
options: All records containing 'Bolt' across all 75,000 items
Only items containing 'Bolt' within the first 2000 fetched records — potentially missing thousands of matches
An error because Search() is not supported on SharePoint
The same result as Filter+StartsWith since they are equivalent on SharePoint
questions.explanation: Search() is non-delegable on SharePoint. It fetches up to the row limit (2000) and searches locally — items beyond record 2000 that contain 'Bolt' will never appear.
wrongExplanations: Search() on SharePoint is non-delegable — it cannot retrieve and search all 75,000 records.
Search() does not throw an error on SharePoint — it silently returns incomplete results.
Search() and Filter+StartsWith behave very differently on SharePoint. Filter+StartsWith is delegable; Search() is not.

questions.q: What is the correct replacement for Search(Inventory, SearchInput.Text, 'ItemName') that is delegation-safe on SharePoint?
options: Filter(Inventory, ItemName = SearchInput.Text)
Filter(Inventory, StartsWith(ItemName, SearchInput.Text))
LookUp(Inventory, ItemName = SearchInput.Text)
Filter(Inventory, EndsWith(ItemName, SearchInput.Text))
questions.explanation: Filter with StartsWith is the delegable text search pattern on SharePoint — the prefix filter is pushed to the server, returning matching records from the full 75,000.
wrongExplanations: Filter with = only matches exact names, not partial text — this is too restrictive for a search bar.
LookUp returns a single record, not a table — not suitable for a gallery displaying multiple search results.
EndsWith is non-delegable on SharePoint — this has the same delegation problem as Search().

msRef.label: StartsWith as a delegable search pattern

questions.q: The CountIf label shows 47 'Out of Stock' items. A manager knows there are over 3,000. What is the most likely cause?
options: The SharePoint list has a column permission issue
CountIf is non-delegable and is only counting within the 2000 fetched records
The Status column is not indexed in SharePoint
The app needs to be republished to refresh the count
questions.explanation: CountIf is non-delegable — it only counts within the locally-fetched record set (2000 in this case). The 3,000+ Out of Stock items beyond record 2000 are never counted.
wrongExplanations: Column permissions would prevent the data loading at all, not produce a partial count.
Column indexing affects query performance, not the delegation row limit for CountIf.
Republishing does not change delegation behaviour. The formula itself must be fixed.

questions.q: The manager uses UpdateIf(Inventory, Selected=true, {Status: 'Discontinued'}) to mark 500 selected items as Discontinued, but only some are updated. Why?
options: UpdateIf requires a premium connector for bulk updates
UpdateIf is non-delegable — it only updates records within the 2000 fetched page
Selected=true is not a valid condition in UpdateIf
Status is a read-only column in SharePoint
questions.explanation: UpdateIf is non-delegable — it evaluates the condition against the locally-fetched page only. Items selected beyond the 2000-record window are not updated.
wrongExplanations: UpdateIf does not require a premium connector for SharePoint.
Selected=true is valid syntax in UpdateIf — the issue is non-delegation, not syntax.
If Status were read-only, UpdateIf would error on all records, not partially succeed.

casestudy.title: The Dataverse Production App
casestudy.scenario: A Power Platform developer is building a customer service canvas app connected to Dataverse with a Contacts table containing 500,000 records. The app requirements are: 1. Search contacts by last name prefix 2. Show contacts assigned to the currently logged-in agent 3. Display total open cases per region (aggregated view) 4. The app must perform correctly at full dataset scale The developer proposes the following formulas: • Contact search: Filter(Contacts, StartsWith(LastName, SearchBox.Text)) • Agent's contacts: Filter(Contacts, AssignedTo = User().Email) • Open cases by region: GroupBy(Filter(Cases, Status='Open'), 'Region', 'RegionCases')

questions.q: The contact search formula Filter(Contacts, StartsWith(LastName, SearchBox.Text)) on Dataverse — how does this perform at 500,000 records?
options: It fetches all 500,000 records to the browser and filters locally — very slow
It delegates to Dataverse — only matching records are returned regardless of dataset size
It throws a delegation warning and returns only 2000 records
It works but requires the LastName column to be indexed first
questions.explanation: Filter + StartsWith fully delegates on Dataverse. The server processes the StartsWith condition and returns only matching contacts — efficient at any scale.
wrongExplanations: With delegation active, Power Apps never downloads all 500,000 records. The server filters before returning.
Filter+StartsWith on Dataverse does not produce a delegation warning — it is a fully supported delegable pattern.
Column indexing affects query performance on the Dataverse server but is not required for delegation to work.

questions.q: Filter(Contacts, AssignedTo = User().Email) on Dataverse — does the User() reference prevent delegation?
options: Yes — User() is evaluated server-side and blocks delegation
No — User().Email is resolved locally to a string and passed as a value in the delegated query
Partially — the filter delegates but only for the first 2000 matching records
No — but only if AssignedTo is a lookup column, not a text column
questions.explanation: User().Email is resolved locally to the current user's email string, then passed as a literal value in the delegated server query. The delegation is not blocked.
wrongExplanations: User() is not evaluated server-side — it runs locally in the app and produces a string that is passed to the server.
There is no 2000-record cap on delegated Filter results — the server returns all matching records.
Column type (lookup vs text) affects the filter syntax but does not change whether User().Email can be used in a delegated query.

questions.q: The open cases by region uses GroupBy(Filter(Cases, Status='Open'), 'Region', 'RegionCases'). What is the issue?
options: Filter is non-delegable on Dataverse
GroupBy is non-delegable — it groups only the fetched record page, potentially missing cases beyond the row limit
Status='Open' comparison is not supported on Dataverse
GroupBy and Filter cannot be combined in a single formula
questions.explanation: Filter delegates on Dataverse, but GroupBy is non-delegable — it groups only the locally-fetched records. If open cases exceed the row limit, the regional totals will be wrong.
wrongExplanations: Filter is fully delegable on Dataverse — it is one of the most reliable delegable functions.
Status='Open' is a valid equality comparison that delegates on Dataverse.
GroupBy and Filter can be combined — the issue is GroupBy's non-delegable nature, not the combination.

casestudy.title: The Exam Architecture Review
casestudy.scenario: A senior developer is reviewing a Power Apps solution built for a financial services firm. The app uses SQL Server as its data source with a Transactions table containing 2 million records. The review identifies the following formulas in the app: • Dashboard total: Sum(Transactions, Amount) • Monthly filter: Filter(Transactions, Month(TransactionDate) = 3) • Recent high-value: FirstN(Sort(Filter(Transactions, Amount > 10000), TransactionDate, Descending), 5) • Customer lookup: LookUp(Transactions, CustomerID = SelectedCustomer.CustomerID) The developer must identify which formulas are safe at 2 million records and which will produce incorrect results.

msRef.label: SQL Server delegation in Power Apps

questions.q: Sum(Transactions, Amount) against SQL Server with 2 million records — what result does this produce?
options: An incorrect partial sum — only the first 500 records are summed
The correct total sum — Sum delegates to SQL Server and processes all 2 million records
An error — Sum is not supported on SQL Server
A correct result but only after a long wait while all records are downloaded
questions.explanation: Sum is one of the aggregation functions that delegates on SQL Server. The server computes the total across all 2 million records and returns a single value — fast and accurate.
wrongExplanations: Sum on SQL Server is delegable — it does not fall back to the row limit.
Sum is specifically supported for delegation on SQL Server — it is one of its delegation advantages.
With delegation, records are never downloaded to the browser. SQL Server computes and returns the sum directly.

questions.q: Filter(Transactions, Month(TransactionDate) = 3) on SQL Server — is this safe?
options: Yes — Filter and Month() both delegate on SQL Server
No — Month() is a non-delegable function; Filter only returns the row-limited page and Month() runs locally
Yes — SQL Server supports all Power Apps functions
Partially — Month() delegates but Filter does not
questions.explanation: While Filter delegates on SQL Server, Month() is not a delegable function — the filter condition runs locally on the fetched page. On 2 million records this will miss most March transactions.
wrongExplanations: Month() is not a delegable function on any data source in Power Apps. The delegation warning appears on Month().
SQL Server supports many delegation scenarios but not every Power Apps formula function.
Filter is delegable but Month() inside the Filter makes the whole condition non-delegable.

questions.q: FirstN(Sort(Filter(Transactions, Amount > 10000), TransactionDate, Descending), 5) — which part prevents safe operation at 2 million records?
options: Filter(Transactions, Amount > 10000) — Filter is non-delegable on SQL Server
FirstN — it is non-delegable and picks from the locally-fetched sorted page
Sort — sorting is not supported on SQL Server
The nesting of three functions — Power Apps does not allow more than two nested functions
questions.explanation: Filter and Sort delegate on SQL Server, but FirstN is non-delegable — it picks the first N from the locally-fetched sorted page. If high-value transactions beyond the row limit exist, they are missed.
wrongExplanations: Filter with Amount > 10000 is fully delegable on SQL Server — comparison operators delegate.
Sort/SortByColumns are delegable on SQL Server — server-side sorting is well-supported.
Power Apps supports deeply nested functions. The issue is specifically FirstN's non-delegable nature.
